questions.stem: An older adult becomes confused, cool, and has a weak pulse after a GI bleed. What is the best bedside interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be unstable from decreased circulating volume
Confusion means the blood loss is not serious
A weak pulse is expected after every bowel problem
The bleeding is probably resolved because the patient is still awake

questions.rationale: Blood loss can reduce circulating volume and cause poor perfusion, confusion, and weak pulses. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Confusion can be a sign of poor perfusion. - C Weak pulse is not a bowel-specific normal finding. - D Awake status does not rule out instability.

distractorRationales.b: "Confusion means the blood loss is not serious" misses the bedside risk because blood loss can reduce circulating volume and cause poor perfusion, confusion points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "The patient may be unstable from decreased circulating volume" fits the safer response to an older adult becomes confused, cool.
distractorRationales.d: "The bleeding is probably resolved because the patient is still awake" misses the bedside risk because bleeding plus confusion and weak pulse can mean unstable circulation points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Bleeding plus confusion and weak pulse can mean unstable circulation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient becomes slower to respond and has trouble keeping their eyes open. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Assess for an acute cause right away
Assume the patient is bored
Wait for the change to pass
Ignore it if the vitals are not extreme

questions.rationale: Slowed responsiveness can signal a neurologic or physiologic change that needs prompt assessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Boredom does not explain an acute change. - C Waiting can miss deterioration. - D Vital signs can look acceptable while the patient worsens.

distractorRationales.b: "Assume the patient is bored" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Slowed responsiveness can signal a neurologic or physiologic change that needs prompt assessment" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Wait for the change to pass" misses the bedside risk because slowed responsiveness can signal a neurologic or physiologic change that needs prompt assessment points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Assess for an acute cause right away" fits the safer response to a patient becomes slower to respond and has trouble keeping their eyes open.

questions.takeaway: Any sudden change in responsiveness deserves attention.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse reviews orders for a patient admitted for an elective knee MRI with IV contrast scheduled for tomorrow. The patient takes metformin 1000 mg BID for type 2 diabetes. Which nursing action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective
Hold metformin beginning the day of and for 48 hours after contrast administration, and notify the ordering provider
Switch the patient to insulin therapy permanently before the procedure
Administer metformin with the morning meal on the day of the procedure and hold only for 24 hours post-contrast

questions.rationale: Metformin is renally cleared. IV iodinated contrast can cause contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN), acutely impairing renal function. If renal function is reduced by CIN, metformin accumulates and raises the risk of lactic acidosis — a rare but potentially fatal complication. Standard protocol: hold metformin on the day of contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then re-assess renal function (creatinine/eGFR) before resuming. Permanent insulin switch is not necessary. Administering on the day of the procedure violates the safety protocol. The provider should be notified to confirm the hold order.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer metformin as scheduled since the procedure is elective" misses the bedside risk because standard protocol: hold metformin on the day of contrast administration and for 48 hours after, then re-assess renal points to unstable glucose physiology. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Permanent insulin switch is not necessary" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "Permanent insulin switch is not necessary" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Metformin + IV contrast: hold the day of and 48h after. Risk is lactic acidosis from metformin accumulation if contrast causes AKI. Check creatinine/eGFR before restarting. This applies to any iodinated contrast study.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A public health nurse is investigating a cluster of foodborne illness cases in a community following a church potluck dinner. What is the correct sequence for outbreak investigation according to epidemiologic principles?

options.text: Implement control measures → identify cases → form hypothesis → test hypothesis
Identify and count cases → establish case definition → form hypothesis about source → implement control measures → test hypothesis
Notify media → quarantine all attendees → collect stool cultures → identify the organism
Form hypothesis first → identify cases → implement controls → notify CDC

questions.rationale: Epidemiologic outbreak investigation sequence (CDC framework): (1) Prepare for fieldwork; (2) Establish existence of an outbreak; (3) Verify diagnosis; (4) Construct a working case definition; (5) Find and count cases (line listing); (6) Perform descriptive epidemiology (person, place, time — epidemic curve); (7) Develop hypotheses; (8) Evaluate hypotheses (case-control or cohort study); (9) Implement control and prevention measures; (10) Communicate findings. Cases are identified and counted before hypotheses are formed; controls are implemented based on evidence, not assumption. Notification goes to appropriate health authorities, not media first.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "(7) Develop hypotheses" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Notify media → quarantine all attendees → collect stool cultures → identify the organism" moves care toward waiting for a callback before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward incomplete escalation instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "(2) Establish existence of an outbreak" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Outbreak investigation: identify cases first → case definition → descriptive epidemiology → hypothesis → test hypothesis → implement controls; evidence-based sequence prevents premature conclusions.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: During a wound assessment, the nurse observes a pressure injury where the base is entirely covered with yellow, adherent slough and the wound depth cannot be determined. The nurse correctly documents this as:

options.text: Stage III pressure injury
Stage IV pressure injury
Unstageable pressure injury
Suspected deep tissue pressure injury

questions.rationale: An unstageable pressure injury is one in which the base is obscured by slough (yellow, tan, gray) or eschar, preventing accurate staging. The wound cannot be assessed as Stage III or IV without debriding the wound bed. Suspected deep tissue injury (D) presents as intact or non-intact skin with deep purple or maroon discoloration or blood-filled blister from underlying soft tissue damage — not covered with slough. Once debrided, the wound can be restaged.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "The wound cannot be assessed as Stage III or IV without debriding the wound bed" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: "Stage IV pressure injury" misses the bedside risk because once debrided, the wound can be restaged points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: Because during a wound assessment, the nurse observes a pressure injury where the base is entirely covered with yellow suggests infection progression, "Suspected deep tissue pressure injury" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.

questions.takeaway: Unstageable = base obscured by slough/eschar, cannot determine depth. Suspected deep tissue injury = intact purple/maroon discoloration.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving IV magnesium sulfate. The nurse assesses: RR 10, deep tendon reflexes absent, urine output 20 mL/hr, serum magnesium level 12 mEq/L. Which action is the immediate priority?

options.text: Decrease the magnesium infusion rate by 50% and continue monitoring
Stop the magnesium infusion and administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly
Notify the provider and document the findings
Increase IV fluid rate to improve urine output and enhance magnesium excretion

questions.rationale: Signs of magnesium toxicity: RR <12/min, absent DTRs, urine output <25 mL/hr, serum Mg >9–10 mEq/L. This patient has multiple toxicity signs (RR 10, absent DTRs, oliguria, Mg 12 mEq/L). The infusion must be stopped immediately. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV (10 mL of 10% solution over 3–5 minutes) is the antidote — calcium antagonizes magnesium's effects. Decreasing the rate (A) is insufficient when toxicity is already established with respiratory compromise. Notifying the provider (C) happens simultaneously but stopping the infusion is the first action. Fluids (D) may help excretion but not fast enough to address impending respiratory arrest.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Stop the magnesium infusion and administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly" fits the safer response to the nurse assesses: RR 10, deep tendon reflexes absent, urine output 20 mL/hr, serum magnesium level 12 mEq/L.
distractorRationales.c: "Notify the provider and document the findings" relies on notification without the bedside safety step. The cue "A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving IV magnesium sulfate" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before waiting for a callback.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Stop the magnesium infusion and administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly" fits the safer response to the nurse assesses: RR 10, deep tendon reflexes absent, urine output 20 mL/hr, serum magnesium level 12 mEq/L.

questions.takeaway: Magnesium toxicity: stop infusion immediately + calcium gluconate 1 g IV as antidote; monitor RR and DTRs every hour during infusion.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient is 6 hours post open abdominal surgery and reports pain rated 8/10. The patient is on scheduled ketorolac IV and a hydromorphone PCA but frequently presses the PCA demand button. Respiratory rate is 14 and SpO2 is 97%. Which is the most appropriate nursing response?

options.text: Contact the physician to increase the PCA demand dose — the patient clearly has inadequate pain control
Withhold all PCA doses and reassess in 2 hours — respiratory rate below 16 requires opioid restriction
Reassess pain using a validated scale, review PCA usage data and lockout history, maximize non-opioid adjuncts (IV acetaminophen if not given), and contact physician if pain remains uncontrolled
Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV prophylactically to protect respiratory reserve before adjusting the PCA

questions.rationale: Effective post-operative pain management requires a systematic, multimodal approach. Steps: (1) Reassess pain thoroughly — PQRST (location, quality, timing — is this expected surgical pain or a new complication like wound dehiscence?); (2) Review PCA data — are demand attempts successful or being locked out? Is the patient pressing correctly? Is the demand dose reaching the lockout ceiling?; (3) Ensure non-opioid adjuncts are maximized: scheduled acetaminophen (reduces opioid requirements 20-30%), ketorolac, and consider regional analgesia; (4) Contact physician if pain remains uncontrolled despite optimized non-opioids. SpO2 97% + RR 14 indicates adequate ventilation — opioids are NOT contraindicated. The outdated practice of withholding opioids for RR <16 without clinical signs of respiratory depression is not evidence-based and results in undertreated pain. Prophylactic naloxone reverses analgesia and is inappropriate without signs of opioid toxicity.

distractorRationales.a: Because steps: (1) Reassess pain thoroughly — PQRST (location, quality, timing — is this expected surgical pain or a suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Contact the physician to increase the PCA demand dose — the patient clearly has inadequate pain control" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.b: "Withhold all PCA doses and reassess in 2 hours — respiratory rate below 16 requires opioid restriction" misses the bedside risk because steps: (1) Reassess pain thoroughly — PQRST (location, quality, timing — is this expected surgical pain or a points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: Because steps: (1) Reassess pain thoroughly — PQRST (location, quality, timing — is this expected surgical pain or a suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV prophylactically to protect respiratory reserve before adjusting the PCA" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Post-op pain: systematic multimodal assessment — maximize non-opioids (acetaminophen, NSAIDs), review PCA data, then escalate to MD. RR 14 + SpO2 97% with no signs of toxicity does NOT justify withholding opioids.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient in a long leg cast following tibial fracture reports severe, unrelenting pain rated 9/10 that is not relieved by opioids, along with paresthesia in the foot and tenseness of the cast. Which action is the NURSE'S PRIORITY?

options.text: Administer the PRN opioid analgesic and elevate the extremity above heart level
Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately
Apply ice pack to reduce swelling and reassess in 30 minutes
Loosen the cast padding and document as a comfort measure

questions.rationale: This presentation suggests acute compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion to the injury, unrelieved by analgesia, and paresthesia (early sign of nerve ischemia). The 6 P's (Pain, Pressure, Paralysis, Paresthesia, Pallor, Pulselessness) guide assessment. The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY — compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6 hours to prevent permanent nerve and muscle damage. Elevating above heart level (A) decreases arterial perfusion, worsening ischemia. Ice (C) and cast loosening (D) delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.a: "Administer the PRN opioid analgesic and elevate the extremity above heart level" misses the bedside risk because compartment syndrome 6 P's: pain out of proportion is the EARLIEST sign points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Assess the 6 P's and notify the provider immediately" fits the safer response to this presentation suggests acute compartment syndrome: pain out of proportion to the injury, unrelieved by analgesia.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "The nurse must notify the provider IMMEDIATELY — compartment syndrome is a surgical emergency requiring fasciotomy within 6" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Compartment syndrome 6 P's: pain out of proportion is the EARLIEST sign. Fasciotomy must occur within 6 hours to prevent permanent damage.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old is admitted with meningococcal meningitis. Which isolation precautions should be initiated immediately, and for how long should they be maintained?

options.text: Contact precautions; maintain until discharge
Droplet precautions; maintain for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy
Airborne precautions in a negative-pressure room; maintain for 48 hours
Standard precautions only; meningococcal meningitis is not transmissible person-to-person

questions.rationale: Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcal meningitis) is transmitted via respiratory droplets (large droplets, short range <3 feet) — not airborne (small droplet nuclei). Droplet precautions are required: surgical mask within 3 feet, private room or cohorting, mask for transport. Duration: droplet precautions are maintained for the first 24 hours after initiation of effective antibiotic therapy (typically ceftriaxone), after which the organism is no longer detectable in respiratory secretions. Close contacts (household members, childcare contacts) should receive prophylaxis with rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Contact precautions; maintain until discharge" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Airborne precautions in a negative-pressure room; maintain for 48 hours" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Meningococcal meningitis: droplet precautions (NOT airborne) for first 24 hours of effective antibiotics" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Meningococcal meningitis: droplet precautions (NOT airborne) for first 24 hours of effective antibiotics; close contacts need chemoprophylaxis (rifampin or ciprofloxacin).

questions.stem: A nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed lithium about safety monitoring. Which statement by the patient indicates correct understanding of lithium toxicity prevention?

options.text: 'I should limit salt in my diet to prevent water retention since lithium can cause edema.'
'I should maintain consistent sodium and fluid intake and notify my doctor if I have diarrhea, vomiting, or excessive sweating.'
'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.'
'Ibuprofen is safe to take for headaches while on lithium.'

questions.rationale: Lithium is excreted by the kidney and competes with sodium for reabsorption. When sodium levels drop (from low-sodium diet, diarrhea, vomiting, sweating, diuretics), the kidney retains lithium — causing toxicity. Consistent sodium and fluid intake, and monitoring for dehydration causes, are essential safety measures. Low-sodium diet (A) increases lithium retention and toxicity risk — the opposite of safe. Abrupt discontinuation (C) causes rebound mania. NSAIDs like ibuprofen (D) decrease renal lithium excretion, raising lithium levels significantly — they are contraindicated.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of renal protection. The stem cue "A nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed lithium about safety monitoring" makes renal injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "'I can stop taking lithium when I feel better, as long as my mood is stable.'" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Lithium is excreted by the kidney and competes with sodium for reabsorption" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of renal protection. The stem cue "A nurse is teaching a patient newly prescribed lithium about safety monitoring" makes renal injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Lithium safety: maintain consistent sodium/fluid intake; avoid NSAIDs (raise lithium levels); report vomiting/diarrhea/excessive sweating immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.
